main_summary.summary: Jack Greenberg, interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Midtown Manhattan on July 18, 2011, recounts his career at the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund (LDF) from 1949 forward. Greenberg describes entering civil rights law through Walter Gellhorn's Columbia Law School seminar 'Legal Survey' (so named to avoid the Communist-tinged 'Civil Rights' label), being recruited by Thurgood Marshall in March 1949 to replace Marian Wynne Perry, and joining a national civil rights bar of roughly six lawyers. He recalls trying the Kansas component of Brown v. Board of Education with Robert L. Carter in Topeka; the dual evidentiary strategy of measurable inequality plus expert testimony on segregation's harm; and the stunned reaction when the May 1954 decision came down. As Director-Counsel from 1961, he describes LDF's representation of roughly 3,500 sit-in defendants, airport-desegregation cases including Coke v. City of Atlanta and A.P. Tureaud's New Orleans case, the cooperating-attorneys network seeded by $10,000 office-startup grants (producing Julius Chambers, Mel Watt, Marian Wright Edelman), Title VII labor-union litigation, Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education's common-law certiorari maneuver against the Nixon-Stennis delay, and Burger's vote-switch in Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education.
key_themes: NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund litigation strategy
Brown v. Board of Education and school desegregation
Civil Rights Act of 1964 implementation through litigation
Supreme Court advocacy and judicial dynamics
main_summary.historical_significance:
Greenberg is one of the last surviving architects of the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund's mid-century litigation campaign and Thurgood Marshall's chosen successor as Director-Counsel (1961-1984). The interview captures first-person testimony on the smallness of the civil rights bar in 1949 (six lawyers nationally), the internal mechanics of trying the Kansas component of Brown, and procedural maneuvers like the common-law writ of certiorari used in Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education to bypass the Nixon administration's Stennis-driven delay strategy.

The testimony also documents the LDF's cooperating-attorneys grant program, which seeded Black legal practices in Southern cities where there were no Black lawyers and which produced figures including Julius Chambers, Mel Watt, and Marian Wright Edelman. Greenberg's account of Chief Justice Earl Warren reporting Burger's conference-vote switch in Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education is a rare direct narration of internal Supreme Court deliberations from a litigant's perspective.

chapters.title: Path to the LDF: Gellhorn's Legal Survey
chapters.summary: Greenberg recounts arriving at Columbia Law School with no civil rights career in mind, taking Professor Walter Gellhorn's seminar 'Legal Survey' (a euphemism chosen to avoid faculty objections in the McCarthy era), and working on actual cases for the few civil-liberties and civil-rights lawyers then practicing. He took the seminar for four semesters and became chair of the student body enrolled in it.
related_events: Walter Gellhorn's congressional testimony on Communist Party membership
Origins of clinical legal education at Columbia

chapters.title: Joining LDF in 1949
chapters.summary: Greenberg describes the size of the civil rights bar in the late 1940s: NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund had three or four lawyers; the ACLU had one; three Jewish organizations had one apiece. After Marian Wynne Perry left LDF to take a New York State position, Walter Gellhorn recommended Greenberg, and Thurgood Marshall hired him in March 1949.
keywords: NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund
related_events: Greenberg's March 1949 hire by Thurgood Marshall
Marian Wynne Perry's transition to New York State

chapters.title: The Marshall Team and LDF Independence
chapters.summary: Greenberg describes the LDF staff he joined: Thurgood Marshall as Director-Counsel after the restrictive-covenant and voting-rights victories, Robert L. Carter as first assistant, Franklin H. Williams (later ambassador to Ghana and head of the Phelps Stokes Foundation), and a staff sociologist. He explains LDF's separation from the parent NAACP in 1957 for tax-exemption reasons and the resulting tensions with Roy Wilkins.
related_events: 1957 LDF tax-exemption-driven separation from NAACP
Franklin H. Williams' ambassadorship to Ghana

chapters.title: Trying the Kansas Component of Brown
chapters.summary: Greenberg and Robert L. Carter tried the Kansas school segregation statute case in Topeka. He explains the dual evidentiary strategy: documenting measurable inequalities in Black versus white schools (books, teachers' salaries, square footage, gymnasia, laboratories) while also urging the court, through expert witnesses, to find that segregation itself constituted inequality.
related_events: 1951 Topeka trial of Brown v. Board of Education
Kansas school segregation statute litigation

chapters.title: Brown Decision and Southern Reaction
chapters.summary: Greenberg recalls the long path from the 1950-51 argument through the 1952 Supreme Court appeal and two reargument cycles to the May 1954 decision. He describes the stunned reaction at LDF and the recognition that Brown swept away all state-imposed segregation, far broader than school segregation alone. He distinguishes the earlier higher-education cases (involving one or two plaintiffs) from school integration's mass-numbers implications, including the segregationist taboo against mixed-sex contact in integrated schools.
related_events: May 17, 1954 Brown v. Board of Education decision
Late-1950s Southern resistance to school desegregation

chapters.title: Sit-In Defense and Race Relations in American Law
chapters.summary: As Director-Counsel from 1961, Greenberg led LDF's representation of approximately 3,500 sit-in defendants, securing acquittals in all of them. He also discusses his 1959 book Race Relations and American Law, the first treatise on civil rights law since approximately 1919. The Columbia Law Review published paired pro and anti reviews, an unusual treatment Greenberg attributes to the topic's perceived controversy.
related_events: 1960 lunch-counter sit-ins beginning in North Carolina
1959 publication of Race Relations and American Law

chapters.title: Critics: Herbert Wechsler and Edmond Cahn
chapters.summary: Greenberg recounts the academic critique of Brown v. Board of Education by Herbert Wechsler in 'Toward Neutral Principles of Constitutional Law' and by NYU legal philosopher Edmond Cahn, who attacked Brown's use of social-science evidence. When Greenberg asked Cahn to review his book, Cahn declined on conflict-of-interest grounds related to his son's interracial marriage.
related_events: Wechsler's 1959 'Toward Neutral Principles of Constitutional Law'
Cahn's critique of Brown's social-science footnote

chapters.title: Airport Desegregation and the Tureaud Story
chapters.summary: Greenberg discusses the segregation of railroad and airport facilities into the 1960s, including at federal facilities north of the Mason-Dixon line. He recounts Coke v. City of Atlanta (desegregating the Atlanta airport in the home city of Coca-Cola) and his case against the New Orleans airport with co-counsel A.P. Tureaud, then the only Black lawyer in New Orleans. After winning, Tureaud took his granddaughter to the airport so she could buy an ice cream cone for the first time.
related_events: Coke v. City of Atlanta airport desegregation case
New Orleans airport desegregation case

chapters.title: Cooperating Attorneys Network
chapters.summary: Greenberg describes the 1963 program he launched to seed Black legal practices in Southern cities that had no Black lawyers. With foundation funding, LDF gave each new attorney $10,000 to buy a typewriter, a rug, and law books. The program produced Julius Chambers in North Carolina, Mel Watt (later a Charlotte congressman), and Marian Wright Edelman, who set up practice in Columbus, Georgia. Greenberg names another beneficiary who became a Georgia congressman from Columbus.
related_events: 1963 LDF launch of Southern cooperating-attorneys grant program
Marian Wright Edelman's Columbus Georgia practice

chapters.title: Civil Rights Act of 1964: Public Accommodations and Employment
chapters.summary: Greenberg recounts LDF's litigation under the Civil Rights Act of 1964: public accommodations (restaurants, theaters, hotels) came easily because businesses wanted the trade, although some hotels created sham private clubs to evade the law. Title VII employment cases proved harder because labor unions, while nationally pro-civil-rights, maintained segregated white and Black locals at the local level. LDF brought some of its 'bitterest cases' against the labor unions.
related_events: 1964 Civil Rights Act enforcement litigation
LDF Title VII union-segregation cases

chapters.title: Alexander v. Holmes and the Common-Law Certiorari Writ
chapters.summary: Greenberg narrates the procedural maneuver in Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education. After Senator John C. Stennis blocked the armed services appropriation bill to pressure Nixon, HEW Secretary Robert H. Finch asked the Fifth Circuit to remand the desegregation cases without ruling. LDF could not appeal because there was no final judgment, so Greenberg invoked the ancient common-law writ of certiorari (distinct from the statutory certiorari) to get the Supreme Court to take the case. The Court agreed and ruled with LDF, ordering immediate desegregation.
keywords: Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education
related_events: 1969 Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education decision
Stennis appropriations blockade
Nixon administration desegregation-delay strategy

chapters.title: Swann and Burger's Vote Switch
chapters.summary: Greenberg describes Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education (1971) as the first substantive case heard under Chief Justice Warren Burger, who wrote an opinion calling for quotas, busing, and teacher integration. Following the argument, the preliminary conference vote favored upholding the lower court against LDF. As conferences continued, Burger found himself outvoted and switched his vote to assign the opinion to himself. Greenberg recounts being told this by Chief Justice Earl Warren, who used the phrase 'there he stood naked.' Burger included a paragraph signaling that desegregation requirements would eventually end, a line that proved consequential 10-15 years later.
keywords: Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education
related_events: 1971 Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education decision
Burger Court's first substantive desegregation ruling

chapters.title: Milliken and the Retreat from Metropolitan Remedies
chapters.summary: Greenberg discusses Milliken v. Bradley (1974) and San Antonio Independent School District v. Rodriguez (1973) as turning points marking the Court's retreat from school-desegregation enforcement. Milliken made political boundaries between city and suburbs determinative for desegregation remedies, separating families living a short distance apart by school-system jurisdiction. Greenberg characterizes the decision as 'really a political decision' rather than a doctrinal one. The interview is cut off mid-discussion of Milliken.
keywords: San Antonio Independent School District v. Rodriguez
related_events: 1974 Milliken v. Bradley decision
1973 San Antonio Independent School District v. Rodriguez decision

loc_video.caption: Jack Greenberg oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in New York, New York, 2011 July 18
